titleStmt.title: Letter from Terrence Duffin to Edward Duffin, 3 December 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Terence Duffin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Terence Duffin to his brother Edward. Terence writes describing the wet and muddy conditions in the trenches. He writes of the General who inspires him with confidence and mentions Captain Day who is the new Brigade Major. Although he qualifies for leave in January but is not sure he will get it.This letter is part of a rich correspondence between various members of the Duffin family, a large prosperous family living in Strandtown, Belfast. Several family members served in the war, including Major Terence Duffin, who served as a staff officer with 107 Brigade, and later with Royal Irish Rifles; Major Charles G Duffin MC, Royal Field Artillery; and their sisters Emma, Celia and Dorothy who served as Voluntary Aid Detachments (VADS) in Egypt and France, and with the YMCA.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Duffin Papers D2109-A, Duffin Papers D2109-A

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
3/12/15 Hd. Qrs 107 Bde B.E.F. My dear Edmund We have just finished with
the cold spell, and the snow which
was quite heavy has disappeared,
also the frost ГўВЂВ” it has now been
raining steadily for two days &
the result is better I'm afraid than
described as far as the trenches are
concerned & you are either up over
your thighs in water or up to your
knees in thick mud ГўВЂВ” fearfully
hard to walk through even, let alone
live in ГўВЂВ” I often wonder how the
men can stand it ГўВЂВ” However the
'Bosch' must be as badly if not
worse off in their part of the line, which
is all we have to comfort ourselves
with! Even though out here
ophone sent out & now can have
music in the evenings! Our General is very cheery
and a splendid man all round
who inspires you with absolute
confidence ГўВЂВ” You know that he
has little to learn as a Brigadier
ГўВЂВ” he has been extraordinary lucky
to have come right through the war
without a scratch especially as
he has been through almost every
fight of importance you could
mention. Capt. Day our new Brigade Major
is a good fellow too ГўВЂВ” an Irishman
from Clones, who was prev-
iously the General's adjutant &
has been with him from the
start ГўВЂВ” With two such experienced
men we should be all right! I hear of big changes in
ГўВЂВ” the 15th Batt. The Colonel has
gone home ГўВЂВ” Gordon is now in
command and the Adjutant
has reverted to Qr Master. You never know whether
your next step will be up or
down! Possibly my next step
will be down ГўВЂВ” I wonder I have
held my present job even as long
as I have! The General goes on leave next
week and Day shortly after
him ГўВЂВ” I qualify about Jan
1st but it does not follow that
I get it. I had rather a sad
letter from poor old Gen. Coachman
yesterday. He is at home with
nothing to do and apparently
feels it deeply ГўВЂВ” It was rather
unkind letting him keep command
even out here and then ordering
him back. I have had no letters
for some days but we have
been told that mails both ways
will be uncertain till after Xmas. I got my winter flannels
safely and was glad to have them. I could do with a decent
pipe as I broke one and the other
is somewhat far through ГўВЂВ” You
might post me out one. Glad to hear Olive will be
home for Xmas ГўВЂВ” I had a line
from her and also from Emma. Best love to all Ever your loving Terry
